name: Direct Response Advertising
description: Ad copy engineered to provoke immediate action — buy, call, click. Roots trace to 1820s patent medicine pamphlets, crystallized by Claude Hopkins and Ogilvy, weaponized online by Kennedy and Halbert.
rules: Lead with the biggest benefit, not the brand
Every sentence must sell the next sentence
Use specific numbers and dated proof over vague claims
Close every piece with a single unambiguous call to action
Address the prospect directly — you, not we
toneNotes: Urgent, confident, benefit-dense. Short sentences. No hedging.

name: Email Marketing
description: The highest-ROI channel in writing. Subject-line science, preheader hooks, narrative nurture sequences, and broadcast blasts that feel like a friend writing.
rules: Subject line wins or loses everything — write 10 and pick one
Open with a pattern interrupt, not a greeting
One goal per email — click, reply, or read
P.S. often outpulls the body — use it
Write like a DM to one person, not a newsletter to thousands
toneNotes: Intimate, conversational, curiosity-driven. Feels like a letter.

name: Long-Form Sales Pages
description: VSLs, webinar replays, landing pages that run 8,000+ words. The architecture Gary Bencivenga, Gene Schwartz, and Dan Kennedy perfected — hook, agitate, reveal, close.
rules: Match the prospect's awareness level — Schwartz five stages
Use story-driven proof, not bulleted feature lists
Stack offer value until the price feels absurdly low
Handle every objection before it forms
Close with stack, guarantee, scarcity, P.S.
toneNotes: Authoritative storyteller. Reads like a 60 Minutes exposé crossed with a sermon.

name: Radio & Podcast Copy
description: Audio-only copy from 1930s radio dramas to host-read podcast ads. Cadence, pause, and voice carry what text cannot.
rules: Write for the ear — read every line aloud
Use contractions and spoken rhythm
Front-load the hook in the first 5 seconds
Let the host's voice breathe — leave white space
End with a memorable URL or offer code
toneNotes: Spoken, warm, trustworthy. Host-as-friend.

name: Brand Manifestos
description: Declaration-style copy that states what the brand stands for and against. Apple Think Different, Nike Crazy Ones, Dove Real Beauty — copy as worldview.
rules: Take a side — a manifesto without enemies is wallpaper
Write in declarations, not descriptions
Use anaphora — repeat the opening phrase
Name the status quo you reject
End with an invitation to join, not a purchase
toneNotes: Prophetic, declarative, rallying. Church-meets-rebellion.

name: Social Hooks (Aggressive)
description: The MrBeast / Alex Hormozi / Pickle school — scroll-stopping, pattern-interrupting, often confrontational opening lines built for the 1.7-second attention window.
rules: First 3 words decide everything
Break a pattern — visual, verbal, or logical
Make a claim so bold it demands verification
Never start with a question the viewer can answer No to
Promise payoff in the hook — deliver it in the body
toneNotes: Loud, confident, provocative. Feels like a slap.

name: Twitter / X Threads
description: Serialized micro-essays where each tweet is a cliffhanger. Built on compression, whitespace, and the dopamine of the next reveal.
rules: Tweet 1 is the entire thread's job — promise plus proof
Every tweet must earn the next click
Use whitespace like a poet — one line, one idea
Number the thread so readers feel progress
End with a bookmarkable summary or offer
toneNotes: Compressed, punchy, authoritative. Every word weighed.

name: Newsletter Writing
description: Morning Brew wit, Substack depth, The Hustle personality. A voice in your inbox that readers look forward to, not delete.
rules: Develop a consistent narrator — voice over brand
Open with a story, not a summary
One big idea per issue, beaten from three angles
Use callbacks to previous issues — build insider intimacy
End with a signature sign-off readers will quote
toneNotes: Warm expert-friend. Smart without showing off.

name: SEO & Blog Content
description: Long-form content that ranks on Google and keeps humans reading. The intersection of search intent, structured data, and narrative craft.
rules: Match search intent before word count
Answer the query in the first 100 words — then expand
Use H2s as standalone answers — featured-snippet-ready
Link internally with descriptive anchor text
Update and date — freshness beats first-mover
toneNotes: Clear, authoritative, scannable. Expert but approachable.

name: Movie Loglines & Pitches
description: The 25-word sell. Protagonist, goal, obstacle, twist — crammed into a sentence that makes a studio exec sit up.
rules: Protagonist plus goal plus obstacle plus stakes — in one sentence
Lead with irony or reversal, not setup
Make the protagonist the worst possible person for the job
Never reveal the third-act twist in the logline
If it sounds familiar, rewrite it — specificity sells
toneNotes: Clinical, ironic, high-concept. Reads like movie-poster tag lines.

name: Screenplay Structure
description: Three-act, Save the Cat, Hero's Journey, Dan Harmon circle. The scaffolds that turn a blank page into 110 pages of movable screen time.
rules: Enter scenes late, leave early
Every scene must change something — character, stakes, or information
Dialogue reveals want — action reveals need
Page count is a promise — act breaks at 25, 50, 75 percent
Subtext over text — what's said is never what's meant
toneNotes: Visual, lean, scene-driven. White space is a character.

name: K-Drama & Short Drama
description: The 16-episode arc that weaponized yearning. Emotional beat precision, slow-burn tension, and the cliffhanger as religion. Now compressed into 60-second vertical drama.
rules: Every episode ends on an emotional cliffhanger, not a plot one
The almost-kiss is more powerful than the kiss
Build a cultural context the audience must learn — then exploit it
Use repetition of a visual motif to anchor feeling
Let silence do 40 percent of the emotional work
toneNotes: Longing, restrained, visually symbolic. Feeling over plot.

name: Theatre & Stage Writing
description: Dialogue that must carry everything because the camera can't move. Monologues, act breaks, the unity of time and place. From Aeschylus to Sorkin.
rules: Every character must sound different on the page
Monologue is earned — never first-act
Write the physical action between the lines
Use repetition and rhythm — theatre is oral tradition
The curtain is a promise of revelation
toneNotes: Elevated, rhythmic, literary. Language aware of itself.

name: Literary Fiction
description: Interiority, prose style, slow-burn tension. Where sentence-level craft is the plot. Marilynne Robinson, Cormac McCarthy, Toni Morrison.
rules: Interiority is plot — what the character notices is the story
Sentence rhythm carries emotional weight
Show the specific — universals emerge from precision
Resist resolution — ambiguity respects the reader
Metaphor earns its place or dies
toneNotes: Measured, observant, layered. Language draws attention to itself when necessary.

name: Thriller & Mystery
description: Page-turn mechanics. Red herrings, ticking clocks, information gaps. Gillian Flynn, Harlan Coben, Lee Child — mechanics that engineer the turn of the page.
rules: End every chapter on a question or complication
Withhold information — but promise the reveal
Plant clues in plain sight, then let noise hide them
Short chapters accelerate pulse — use them at Act 3
The antagonist's logic must be airtight
toneNotes: Propulsive, economical, suspenseful. Sentences clipped for pace.

name: Romance (All Subgenres)
description: Regency, contemporary, dark romance, romantasy. The genre that mastered the almost — tension, yearning, the guaranteed emotional payoff of HEA.
rules: Promise the HEA or HFN — readers signed a contract
The almost-touch outsells the touch
Build tension through denial and interruption
POV shifts reveal the unspoken want
Tropes are load-bearing — subvert carefully, never mock
toneNotes: Yearning, charged, emotional. Internal stakes over external.

name: Horror Writing
description: The unseen scares more than the seen. Dread architecture from Shirley Jackson to Stephen King to Mariana Enriquez.
rules: The dread is in the wait, not the reveal
Ground the impossible in the mundane first
Unreliable perception is the cheapest and deepest terror
Use concrete sensory detail — never vague fear
The ending must earn the dread — no bait-and-switch
toneNotes: Creeping, sensory, atmospheric. Dread accumulates, terror punctuates.

name: Fantasy & Science Fiction
description: World-building without exposition dumps. Magic systems, speculative tech, rules that matter. Sanderson, Le Guin, Ted Chiang.
rules: Rules of the world must be consistent — break them with cost
Reveal world through character behavior, not narration
One unfamiliar concept per page — no more
Exposition hides inside conflict or humor
The speculative element must mean something thematically
toneNotes: Immersive, curious, precise. World is character.

name: Children's & YA
description: Voice-driven identity fiction. Sharp stakes, earned emotion, no condescension. From Roald Dahl to Judy Blume to Angie Thomas.
rules: Never write down — kids smell condescension
Voice is the whole game — protagonists sound like themselves
Stakes feel life-or-death because to the protagonist they are
Adults are obstacles, allies, or absent — never solutions
Identity question drives plot — who am I becoming
toneNotes: Direct, immediate, emotionally literate. First-person dominant.

name: Self-Help Nonfiction
description: Promise, problem, mechanism, solution, proof. The architecture of Dale Carnegie through Atomic Habits.
rules: Promise transformation in the title and first page
Name the problem with more precision than the reader could themselves
Introduce a mechanism or frame — give it a name
Prove with story, not statistics
Close every chapter with a specific action the reader can take tonight
toneNotes: Confident teacher. Authoritative without condescension.

name: Business & Narrative Nonfiction
description: Gladwell, Lewis, Susan Cain. Ideas delivered as stories. Anecdote-first, principle second, evidence stack as structure.
rules: Open every chapter with a specific scene, not a thesis
The principle arrives as revelation, not introduction
Stack three proofs minimum per major claim
Characters carry ideas — no disembodied concepts
The idea must reframe something familiar
toneNotes: Curious, narrative-driven, reframing. Reads like a magazine feature that earned 300 pages.

name: True Crime & Longform Journalism
description: Capote's In Cold Blood to Serial to David Grann. Facts told with novelistic craft — scene, dialogue, interiority built from the record.
rules: Every detail must be sourced — attribution is the spine
Scene-build from the record — never invent
Withhold strategically — mirror the investigator's timeline
Humanize the victim beyond their death
Ethical stakes are as real as narrative stakes
toneNotes: Restrained, meticulous, morally alert. Facts carry the weight.

name: Speeches & Rhetoric
description: Cicero to Lincoln to Sorkin. Ethos, pathos, logos. Rhetorical devices — anaphora, tricolon, antithesis — engineered for voice and memory.
rules: Write for the ear, not the page — read aloud until rhythm locks
Use tricolon — three-beat phrases land harder
Anaphora turns lines into chants
Antithesis creates memorability
End on a specific image, not an abstract call
toneNotes: Rhythmic, repetitive in structure, elevated. Language meant to be spoken.

name: Poetry
description: Compression, rhythm, image. The oldest form of NLP — anchoring emotion through sound and structure. From sonnets to Ocean Vuong.
rules: Every word audited — no passengers
Sound is meaning — assonance, consonance, stress pattern
Concrete image beats abstraction every time
Line break is punctuation — use it deliberately
Resist explaining the poem inside the poem
toneNotes: Compressed, sonic, image-rich. Silence as much as speech.

name: Hook Taxonomy
description: The 25 plus repeatable hook types that open any piece of writing — curiosity gap, pattern interrupt, identity hook, contrarian hook, data shock, story open, embedded command, open loop, and more.
rules: Name the hook type before you write it
Match the hook to the awareness level of the reader
Never combine more than two hook types in one opening
Every hook sets up a promise — keep it or lose the reader
Test three hooks before committing to one
toneNotes: Tactical, variable. Hook type dictates tone.

name: Branded Social Voice
description: Duolingo chaotic, Wendy's roast, Ryanair deadpan. A consistent brand persona that feels like a person, not a logo, across every platform.
rules: Voice is a character with a backstory — write the bible first
Pick what the brand will never say — constraints define voice
Reply like a person, not a help desk
Humor must risk offense to land — sanitized is invisible
Consistency across posts matters more than cleverness of any one
toneNotes: Varies by brand — but always specific, consistent, human.
